problem_md: $ABCD$ is a square. $P$ is a point inside $ABCD$ such that $\angle APD + \angle BPC = 180^\circ$ and $\angle BPC$ is acute. If $PB = 3$ and $PC = 4$, find $BC$.
solution_md:
Answer: $1 + 2\sqrt{2}$

We translate $\triangle APD$ to $\triangle BQC$ so that the condition becomes $\angle BQC + \angle BPC = 180^\circ$. This means $BQCP$ is a cyclic quadrilateral. Draw a line through $P$ parallel to $AB$. This line passes through $Q$, and let $E$ and $F$ be the points where this line meets $AD$ and $BC$ respectively.

Note that $AB = PQ$ due to the translation. Hence
$BC = PQ$, so they subtend equal angles on the circumference of the circle.

Equating the angles subtended on the major arcs gives $\angle BPC = \angle QCP$ (note that $\angle BPC$ is given to be acute, and that $\angle QCP$ is acute since $PB < PC$). Equating the angles subtended on the minor arcs gives $\angle BQC = \angle PBQ$. Since we also have $\angle BQC + \angle BPC = 180^\circ$, it follows that the cyclic quadrilateral $BQCP$ is actually an isosceles trapezoid with $BQ$ and $PC$ parallel. Hence we have $FP = FC = 2\sqrt{2}$, and so $BF = \sqrt{3^2 - (2\sqrt{2})^2} = 1$. It follows that $BC = BF + FC = 1 + 2\sqrt{2}$.

problem_md: In a chess tournament, there were $n$ contestants and any two of them played at most one game against each other. Each contestant has played exactly $100$ games. For any two contestants $A$ and $B$ who had played against each other, there were exactly $50$ other participants who have played against both $A$ and $B$. On the other hand, for any two contestants $C$ and $D$ who had not played against each other, there were exactly $4$ other participants who have played against both $C$ and $D$. Find the value of $n$.
solution_md:
Answer: $1326$

Pick any contestant $x$. Suppose he has played against $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{100}$ (call these Group Y contestants). Denote the contestants who have not played against $x$ by $z_1, z_2, \dots, z_m$ (call these Group Z contestants).

![](attached_image_1.png)

A Group Y contestant (who has played against $x$) has exactly $50$ common opponents with $x$ (who must be Group Y contestants), and so has played against exactly $100 - 1 - 50 = 49$ Group Z contestants. On the other hand, since a Group Z contestant has not played against $x$, he has exactly $4$ common opponents with $x$ (who must be Group Y contestants). Thus the number of games between a Group Y contestant and a Group Z contestant is equal to $100 \times 49$ as well as $4m$. It follows that these numbers are equal, and so
$$
n = 1 + 100 + m = 1 + 100 + \frac{100 \times 49}{4} = 1326.
$$
The scenario described in the question is indeed possible. Note that the answer is actually $\binom{52}{2}$. To construct the scenario, label the contestants by $2$-element subsets of $\{1, 2, \dots, 52\}$, and let two contestants play a game if and only if their corresponding subsets have a common element. It is easy to check that all given conditions are satisfied.

problem_md: There are 20 students in a class, numbered $1$ to $20$. They have taken a test with $n$ questions. After the test, the teacher tried to pick some students to draft the solutions, and as such it is necessary that the picked students together can solve all questions. The teacher found that this can be only done by choosing all students whose class numbers are even, or all students whose class numbers are multiples of $5$, or any combination that includes all students from one of the two groups mentioned above. Find the smallest possible value of $n$.
solution_md:
Answer: $18$

For convenience we shall call 'a student whose class number is even' an even student. We make the following observations.

(1) Every question is solved by at least one even student (as the teacher could choose all even students).

(2) Similarly, every question is solved by at least one of students $5$, $10$, $15$, $20$.

(3) Students $2$, $4$, $6$, $\ldots$, $18$ (i.e. all even students except student $20$) cannot solve all questions (since any choice of the teacher must include either all even students or all students whose numbers are multiples of $5$). By (1), there must be a question which was solved by student $20$ but not by any other even student.

(4) For the same reason in (3), there must be a question which was solved by student $10$ but not by any other even student.

(5) We claim that there exist at least two questions that were solved by student $2$ but not any other even student. Using the same argument as in (3), we can show that there is at least one such question. Now suppose there is exactly one such question $P$. Now neither student $10$ nor $20$ solved $P$, so by (2) we know that either student $5$ or $15$ solved $P$. Without loss of generality suppose student $5$ did it. Consider students $4$, $5$, $6$, $8$, $10$, $12$, $14$, $16$, $18$, $20$ (i.e. all even students but with student $2$ replaced by student $5$). Since student $2$ and student $15$ are missing, these students together did not solve all questions. That means there is a question $Q$ which cannot be solved by these students (note that $P \neq Q$ since student $5$ solved $P$). As student $2$ cannot solve $Q$ either (since $P$ is the only question solved by student $2$ but not any other even student), that means no even student could solve $Q$, which contradicts (1). This proves the claim.

(6) For the same reason in (5), for each of students $4$, $6$, $8$, $12$, $14$, $16$, $18$, there must be at least two questions that were solved by that student but not any other even student.

From (3), (4), (5) and (6), there are at least $1 + 1 + 2 + 2 \times 7 = 18$ questions, and they are clearly distinct (by considering which even student solved each question). On the other hand, we can construct a paper with exactly $18$ questions as follows:

* Question $10$ ŌĆö solved by student $10$ only
* Question $20$ ŌĆö solved by student $20$ only
* Question $x$ for each $x \in \{2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 14, 16, 18\}$ ŌĆö solved by students $5$ and $x$ only
* Question $x'$ for each $x' \in \{2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 14, 16, 18\}$ ŌĆö solved by students $15$ and $x$ only

It can be easily checked that all even students together solved all questions, all students whose numbers are multiples of $5$ together solved all questions, and that if not all even students are present and not all students whose numbers are multiples of $5$ are present, then at least one question is not solved by the students picked. The answer is thus $18$.

problem_md: Given $\triangle ABC$, $\angle CAB = 75^\circ$ and $\angle ACB = 45^\circ$. $BC$ is extended to $T$ so that $BC = CT$. Let $M$ be the midpoint of the segment $AT$. Find $\angle BMC$.
solution_md:
As $M$ and $C$ are midpoints of $AT$ and $BT$ respectively, we have $MC // AB$ and $MC = \frac{1}{2} AB$ by the midpoint theorem. Extend $CM$ to $K$ so that $CM = MK$. Then $AB = CK$, and hence $ABCK$ is a parallelogram. Also, extend $CA$ to $S$ so that $\angle SKC = 75^\circ$. Then $\triangle SKC$ is an isosceles triangle with $SC = SK$. Therefore, $\angle SMC = \angle SMK = 90^\circ$.

Let $BA$ meet $SK$ at $L$. Then $LKCA$ is an isosceles trapezoid. It follows that $LC = AK = BC$. Together with $\angle B = 60^\circ$, we know that $\triangle CBL$ is equilateral, and hence $BC = BL$. Furthermore, $\angle LSC = 30^\circ = \frac{1}{2} \angle LBC$. Thus, $S$ lies on the circle with centre $B$ and radius $BC$. Therefore, $BS = BL = BC$, and
$$
\angle SBC = 180^\circ - 2\angle BCA = 90^\circ.
$$
Finally, $S, B, C, M$ are concyclic since $\angle SBC + \angle SMC = 180^\circ$. It follows that $\angle BMC = \angle BSC = 45^\circ$.

problem_md: We say that a real number is 'almost an integer' if it differs from an integer by at most $0.1$. For example, $2023$, $-2023.9$ and $2023.0822$ are almost integers. Show that among any $10$ real numbers, there exist two different real numbers whose difference is almost an integer.
solution_md:
Let $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{10}$ be the real numbers. For each $j$, we write $x_j = a_j + b_j$ where $a_j$ is the largest integer not exceeding $x_j$. Note that we must have $0 \le b_j < 1$. WLOG we may assume $b_1 \le b_2 \le \dots \le b_{10}$.

* If $b_{j+1} - b_j \le 0.1$ for some $j = 1, 2, \dots, 9$, then $x_{j+1} - x_j$ is almost an integer since
$$
(x_{j+1} - x_j) - (a_{j+1} - a_j) = b_{j+1} - b_j \in [0, 0.1],
$$
where $a_{j+1} - a_j$ is an integer.

* If $b_{j+1} - b_j > 0.1$ for all $j = 1, 2, \dots, 9$, then $b_{10} - b_1 > 0.9$, and hence $x_1 - x_{10}$ is almost an integer since
$$
(x_1 - x_{10}) - (a_1 - a_{10} - 1) = 1 - (b_{10} - b_1) \in (0, 0.1),
$$
where $a_1 - a_{10} - 1$ is an integer.

Therefore, in any case, we can find two different real numbers whose difference is almost an integer.

problem_md: Let $n$ be a positive integer. In each cell of an $n \times n$ grid, we place a coin with either the head or the tail facing upwards. Each time we can select one cell, and flip all the $2^n - 1$ coins that lie in the same row or the same column as this cell. Find all $n$ such that it is always possible to have all the coins with the heads facing upwards after finitely many moves, no matter how we place the coins initially.
solution_md:
Answer: 1 and all positive even numbers.

In the following, to operate on the $(i, j)$th cell (the cell in the $i$th row and the $j$th column) means to perform an operation by selecting the $(i, j)$th cell in the operation.

* When $n = 1$, it is clear that the goal can always be met.

* When $n$ is even, it suffices to show that it is possible to flip any arbitrary coin only in some moves. Indeed, if we want to flip the coin in the $(i, j)$th cell (called $C$), we simply operate on every cell that lies in the same row or the same column as $C$.

- The coin in $C$ is flipped $2n - 1$ times, which is odd.
- Each coin in the same row or the same column as $C$ other than the coin in $C$ is flipped $n$ times, which is even.
- Every other coin is flipped $2$ times, which is even.

Therefore, we see that only the coin in $C$ is flipped after these operations.

* When $n$ is odd and greater than $1$, it suffices to consider the reverse process, i.e. starting with all coins with the heads facing upwards, we cannot obtain all possible configurations after finitely many moves. Note that there are $2^{n^2}$ possible configurations since there are two possibilities for each of the $n^2$ cells. On the other hand, note that the order of operations does not affect the result, and operating the same cell twice does not change the result. Therefore, we may assume each cell is operated $0$ or $1$ time. This leads to $2^{n^2}$ different sets of operations. Thus, it suffices to show that there are two different sets of operations that lead to the same configuration.

Indeed, we simply operate on every cell in the first row and the first column except the $(1, 1)$th cell.
- The coin in the $(1, 1)$th cell is flipped $2(n-1)$ times, which is even.
- Each coin in the first row or the first column other than the coin in the $(1, 1)$th cell is flipped $n-1$ times, which is even.
- Every other coin is flipped $2$ times, which is even.

Therefore, we see that no coin is flipped after these operations, which means this has the same effect as not performing any operation at all (which is also one of the $2^{n^2}$ sets of operations). This completes the proof.

problem_md: Let $n$ be a positive integer. Show that if $p$ is a prime dividing $5^{4n} - 5^{3n} + 5^{2n} - 5^n + 1$, then $p \equiv 1 \pmod 4$.
solution_md:
Clearly, $p \neq 2, 5$. Let $m = 5^{4n} - 5^{3n} + 5^{2n} - 5^n + 1$. Then
$$
(2 \cdot 5^{2n} - 5^n + 2)^2 - 5 \cdot 5^{2n} = 4m \equiv 0 \pmod{p}.
$$
This gives $5 \equiv (5^{-n}(2 \cdot 5^{2n} - 5^n + 2))^2 \pmod{p}$. Using the Legendre symbol, we have $\left(\frac{5}{p}\right) = 1$. On the other hand, we have
$$
(5^{2n} - 5^n + 1)^2 + 5^n(5^n - 1)^2 = m \equiv 0 \pmod{p}.
$$
As above, this implies $\left(\frac{-5^n}{p}\right) = 1$. It follows that
$$
\left(\frac{-1}{p}\right) = \left(\frac{-5^n}{p}\right) \left(\frac{5^n}{p}\right) = 1 \cdot 1^n = 1.
$$
Therefore, $p \equiv 1 \pmod 4$.

problem_md: Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers such that $\sqrt{m} + \sqrt{n} = \sqrt{2023}$. Find the greatest possible value of $m+n$.
solution_md:
Note that $\sqrt{2023} = 17\sqrt{7}$. Hence we must have $\sqrt{m} = p\sqrt{7}$ and $\sqrt{n} = q\sqrt{7}$, where $p$ and $q$ are positive integers with sum $17$. We have
$$
m + n = 7p^2 + 7q^2 = 7p^2 + 7(17-p)^2 = 14 \left[ \left( p - \frac{17}{2} \right)^2 + \frac{289}{4} \right],
$$
which is maximized when $p$ is as far away from $\frac{17}{2}$ as possible. We should take either $(p, q) = (1, 16)$ or $(16, 1)$, and the answer is thus $7(1)^2 + 7(16)^2 = 1799$.

problem_md: Given an acute $\triangle ABC$ with $AB > AC$, let $M$ be the midpoint of the side $BC$. Let $I$ be the incentre of $\triangle ABC$, and let $D$ be the foot of perpendicular from $I$ to $BC$. The line passing through $A$ and perpendicular to $BC$ meets the circumcircle of $\triangle ABC$ again at $E \neq A$. Suppose $\angle MID = \angle ACB - \angle ABC$. Prove that $DE = DI$.
solution_md:
Let $O$ and $H$ be the circumcentre and the orthocentre of $\triangle ABC$ respectively. Reflect $A$ about $O$ to a point $A'$. Reflect $D$ about $M$ and $I$ to points $D'$ and $P$ respectively. Also, let $Q$ be the midpoint of $AH$. Notice the following well-known results.

* $E$ and $H$ are symmetric about the line $BC$
* $M$ is the midpoint of $A'H$
* $A$, $P$, $D'$ are collinear (as $D'$ is the contact point of the $A$-excircle and the side $BC$)

$$
\angle OAI = \angle HAI
$$

Now, we first find that $\angle OAH = A - (90^\circ - C) - (90^\circ - C) = C - B = \angle MID$. As $AH \parallel ID$, we have $AO \parallel IM$. By the midpoint theorem, we also have $APD' \parallel IM$. Therefore, $A$, $P$, $O$, $D'$, $A'$ are collinear. Again by the midpoint theorem, we have $QM \parallel AA'$, and hence $Q$, $I$, $M$ are collinear. This shows $APIQ$ is a parallelogram. But since $AI$ intersects $\angle PAQ$, the parallelogram $APIQ$ is indeed a rhombus. In addition, as $ID = IP$ and $QH = QA$, $IDHQ$ is also a rhombus. It follows that $DE = DH = DI$.

title: ķ”ÖµĖ» ???? #448
problem_md: Let $u, v, w$ be positive integers, with $v$ and $w$ not exceeding $2023$, such that $u - v = 2w$ and $(u^2 + w^2)(v^2 + w^2)$ is divisible by $289$. How many sets of possible values of $(u, v, w)$ are there?
solution_md:
Answer: $67473$

It suffices to choose $v$ and $w$ since there is a unique choice for $u = v + 2w$ after fixing $v$ and $w$. Note that $289 = 17^2$ and $2023 = 7 \times 17^2$. We consider two cases.

Case 1: $w$ is divisible by $17$

In this case $v$ is also divisible by $17$ (if not, then $u = v + 2w$ not divisible by $17$, so neither $u^2 + w^2$ nor $v^2 + w^2$ can be divisible by $17$). Furthermore, any choice of positive multiples of $17$ not exceeding $2023$ for each of $v$ and $w$ satisfies the given condition. Hence there are $7 \times 17$ choices for each of $v$ and $w$, and thus $(7 \times 17)^2$ sets of $(u, v, w)$ in this case.

Case 2: $w$ is not divisible by $17$

There are $2023 - 7 \times 17 = 7 \times 17 \times 16$ choices for $w$. For each $w$, we try to find $v$. First note that
$$
\begin{aligned}
(u^2 + w^2)(v^2 + w^2) &\equiv (u^2 - 16w^2)(v^2 - 16w^2) \\
&= (u + 4w)(u - 4w)(v + 4w)(v - 4w) \\
&= (v + 6w)(v - 2w)(v + 4w)(v - 4w) \pmod{17}.
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence we can choose $v$ as follows.

* From the above we see that $v$ must be equal to $-6w, 2w, -4w$ or $4w$ modulo $17$ (and they are distinct modulo $17$).
* Each of the 4 choices of $v$ modulo $17$ corresponds to exactly one choice of $v$ modulo $289$ for which $(u^2 + w^2)(v^2 + w^2)$ is divisible by $289$.
* Each choice of $v$ modulo $289$ corresponds to $7$ actual possible values of $v$ since $2023 = 7 \times 289$.

Hence for each $w$, there are $4 \times 7$ choices for $v$. It follows that there are altogether $(7 \times 17 \times 16) \times (4 \times 7) = 7^2 \times 17 \times 64$ sets of $(u, v, w)$ in this case.

It follows that the answer is $(7 \times 17)^2 + 7^2 \times 17 \times 64 = 7^2 \times 17 \times 81 = 67473$.

problem_md:
There are $2n$ cards labelled $1, 2, \ldots, 2n$ respectively, where $n$ is a positive integer. Two players $A$ and $B$ take turns choosing a card which has not been chosen by anyone, with $A$ going first, until all cards are chosen. Let $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$ be the labels of the cards chosen by $A$ in this order, and let $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n$ be the labels of the cards chosen by $B$ in this order. The score of $A$ is equal to
$$
(a_1 + a_2 + \dots + a_n)^2 + (b_1 + b_2 + \dots + b_n)^2 - (a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + \dots + a_nb_n).
$$
What is the maximum score (in terms of $n$) that $A$ can guarantee to obtain regardless of how $B$ plays?
solution_md:
The answer is
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &:= \left( \sum_{k=1}^{n} 2k \right)^2 + \left( \sum_{k=1}^{n} (2k-1) \right)^2 - \sum_{k=1}^{n} (2k-1)(2k) \\
&= n^2(n+1)^2 + n^4 - \left( \frac{2n(n+1)(2n+1)}{3} - n(n+1) \right) \\
&= \frac{n(6n^3 + 2n^2 + 1)}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Denote
$X = a_1 + a_2 + \cdots + a_n,$
$Y = b_1 + b_2 + \cdots + b_n,$
$S = X^2 + Y^2,$
$T = a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2 + \cdots + a_n b_n.$

Claim. $T \le 1 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 4 + \cdots + (2n-1)(2n)$

*Proof.* Since there are finitely many possible ways to assign the values, $T$ must attain a maximum value. For this maximal case, we may assume $a_i < b_i$ for each $i$. Note that we may assume $\{a_i\}$ and $\{b_i\}$ are increasing by the rearrangement inequality.

Under these assumptions, we must have $a_1 = 1$. We are going to show that $a_i = 2i - 1$ and $b_i = 2i$ for all $i$. Suppose on the contrary that this does not hold. Then there is a smallest index $k$ such that $b_k \neq 2k$. Note that we must have $a_i = 2i - 1$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, k$ and $a_{k+1} = 2k$ (clearly $k \neq n$). This implies $a_k < a_{k+1} < b_k < b_{k+1}$. By the rearrangement inequality, we have
$$
a_k b_k + b_{k+1} a_{k+1} < a_k a_{k+1} + b_{k+1} b_k.
$$
This means we can swap $b_k$ and $a_{k+1}$ to increase the value of $T$, contradiction.

Therefore, $T$ is maximized when $a_i = 2i - 1$ and $b_i = 2i$ for all $i$. This proves the claim. $\square$

Now, we show that $A$ can guarantee to obtain score $N$. A strategy is to choose the card with the largest label in each round. Note that $a_i \ge 2(n+1-i)$ for each $i$ since at least one of the cards $2(n+1-i), 2(n+1-i)+1, \dots, 2n$ is not chosen in the $i$th round. As $X+Y = 1+2+\dots+2n$ is fixed and $X > Y$ (because of this strategy), $S$ is smaller when $X$ is smaller. Thus,
$$
S \ge (2+4+\dots+2n)^2 + (1+3+\dots+(2n-1))^2.
$$
Together with the claim, we deduce
$$
S - T \ge \left( \sum_{k=1}^{n} 2k \right)^2 + \left( \sum_{k=1}^{n} (2k-1) \right)^2 - \sum_{k=1}^{n} (2k-1)(2k) = N
$$
as desired.

Next, we show that $B$ can guarantee that the score of $A$ is at most $N$. A strategy is to pair up the cards with labels $2m-1$ and $2m$ for each $m = 1, 2, \dots, n$, and choose a card in the same pair as the card just chosen by $A$ in each round. It is easy to see that
$$
T = 1 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 4 + \dots + (2n-1)(2n).
$$
Also, as $|a_i - b_i| = 1$ for each $i$, we have
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= X^2 + Y^2 = \frac{(X+Y)^2 + (X-Y)^2}{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \left[ (1+2+\dots+2n)^2 + \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} (a_i - b_i) \right)^2 \right] \\
&\le \frac{(1+2+\dots+2n)^2 + n^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equality holds when $a_i - b_i = 1$ for all $i$ or $a_i - b_i = -1$ for all $i$. This is exactly the case when
$$
S-T = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} 2k\right)^2 + \left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} (2k-1)\right)^2 - \sum_{k=1}^{n} (2k-1)(2k) = N.
$$
The proof is complete.

problem_md: Given $\triangle ABC$ with $AB < AC$, let $AD$ be the bisector of $\angle BAC$ with $D$ on the side $BC$. Let $\Gamma$ be a circle passing through $A$ and $D$ which is tangent to $BC$ at $D$. Suppose $\Gamma$ cuts the side $AB$ again at $E \neq A$. The tangent to the circumcircle of $\triangle BDE$ at $D$ intersects $\Gamma$ again at $F \neq D$. Let $P$ be the intersection point of the segments $EF$ and $AC$. Prove that $PD$ is perpendicular to $BC$.
solution_md:
Let $Q \neq A$ be the second intersection point of $\Gamma$ and $AC$. As
$$
\angle EDB = \angle EAD = \angle DAQ = \angle DEQ,
$$
we have $EQ // BC$. Also, we have $AF // BC$ since
$$
\angle EAF = 180^\circ - \angle FDE = 180^\circ - \angle DBE.
$$

This shows $EQ // AF$, and hence $AEQF$ is an isosceles trapezoid. Thus, the intersection point $P$ of the diagonals lies on the perpendicular bisector of $EQ$. Note that $D$ also lies on the perpendicular bisector of $EQ$ because $AD$ bisects $\angle EAQ$ and $A$, $E$, $D$, $Q$ are concyclic. Therefore, $PD \perp EQ$, which implies $PD \perp BC$.

problem_md:
Let $n$ be a positive integer. Prove that there exists a positive integer $m$ such that
$$
3^n + 5^n - 1
$$
is divisible by $7^n$.
solution_md:
We show that $m = 7^{n-1}$ works. By the lifting the exponent lemma, we have
$$
v_7(3^m + 4^m) = v_7(3^m + 4^m) + v_7(m) = 1 + (n-1) = n.
$$
This implies $7^m \mid 3^m + 4^m$, and hence $3^m \equiv -4^m \pmod{7^n}$. Similarly, $5^m \equiv -2^m \pmod{7^n}$. It follows that
$$
3^m + 5^m - 1 \equiv -4^m - 2^m - 1 \equiv -(2^{2m} + 2^m + 1) \pmod{7^n}. \quad (1)
$$
Next, again by the lifting the exponent lemma, we have
$$
v_7(8^m - 1) = v_7(8^m - 1) + v_7(m) = 1 + (n-1) = n.
$$
This implies $7^m \mid 8^m - 1 = (2^m - 1)(2^{2m} + 2^m + 1)$. As $3 \nmid m$, we have $7 \nmid 2^m - 1$. Therefore, we must have
$$
7^m \mid 2^{2m} + 2^m + 1. \quad (2)
$$
Combining (1) and (2), we see that $7^m \mid 3^m + 5^m - 1$ as desired.

problem_md: A point $P$ lies inside an equilateral triangle $ABC$ such that $AP = 15$ and $BP = 8$. Find the maximum possible value of the sum of areas of triangles $ABP$ and $BCP$.
solution_md:
Rotate $\triangle PBC$ about point $B$ by $60^\circ$ in the anticlockwise direction, so that $BA$ is the image of $BC$ after rotation. Let $Q$ be the image of $P$ after rotation. Then
$$
[ABP] + [BCP] = [PAQB] = [PQB] + [QPA].
$$

![](attached_image_1.png)

Now, $\triangle PQB$ is an equilateral triangle with side length $8$, and so its area is
$$
\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times 8^2 = 16\sqrt{3}.
$$
Also, we have
$$
[QPA] = \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 15 \times \sin \angle QPA \le \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 15 = 60.
$$
Thus, the sum of areas is at most $60 + 16\sqrt{3}$. Equality holds when $\angle QPA = 90^\circ$, i.e. $\angle BPA = 150^\circ$ (which fixes $\triangle ABC$ and point $P$).

problem_md: There is a $20 \times 20$ table and $400$ cards printed with the numbers $1$ to $400$. The cards are then distributed to the cells of the table so that there is one card in each cell. After that, we put a red sticker on the card in each row with the largest number, and a blue sticker on the card in each column with the largest number. Let $A$ denote the smallest number on the $20$ cards with red stickers, and $B$ denote the smallest number on the $20$ cards with blue stickers. How many different possible values of $|A - B|$ are there?
solution_md:
Answer: $362$

For convenience we shall use the term 'red number' to the number on a card with a red sticker, and similarly for 'blue number'. Clearly, a red number, being a row maximum, is at least $20$. Also, since $A$ is the smallest red number, there are $19$ other red numbers greater than $A$, so $A$ is at most $381$. The same is true for $B$. That is, both $A$ and $B$ are between $20$ and $381$ (inclusive), and so $|A - B|$ is at most $361$.

We shall prove that all these $362$ possibilities (from $0$ to $361$) for $|A - B|$ are attainable. Indeed, we can make $A = 381$ and $B$ can be equal to any value between $20$ and $381$. To make $A = B = 381$ we can use the following configuration (the arrangements of the starred entries are unimportant):

| 381 | * | * | ... | * |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| * | 382 | * | ... | * |
| * | * | 383 | ... | * |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| * | * | * | ... | 400 |

On the other hand for any $B \in \{20, 21, ..., 380\}$, we can use the following configuration to obtain such value of $B$ while having $A = 381$:

| 1 | * | * | ... | * | 381 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2 | 382 | * | ... | * | * |
| 3 | * | 383 | ... | * | * |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 19 | * | * | ... | 399 | * |
| B | * | * | ... | * | 400 |

It follows that the answer is $362$.

problem_md:
Let $n \ge 4$ be a positive integer. Consider any set $A$ formed by $n$ distinct real numbers such that the following condition holds: for every $a \in A$, there exist distinct elements $x, y, z \in A$ such that $|x - a|, |y - a|, |z - a| \ge 1$. For each $n$, find the greatest real number $M$ such that
$$
\sum_{a \in A} |a| \ge M
$$
always holds.
solution_md:
The greatest $M$ is $4$ if $n = 4$, and is $3$ if $n \ge 5$.

Let $S$ be the sum $\sum_{a \in A} |a|$.

For $n = 4$, by considering $A = \{-1, 0, 1, 2\}$, we need $M \le 4$. Let $a < b < c < d$ be the elements in $A$. From the condition, we must have $b - a, c - b, d - c \ge 1$. This implies
$$
S = (|a| + |d|) + (|b| + |c|) \ge |d - a| + |c - b| \ge 3 + 1 = 4.
$$
This shows the largest $M$ is $4$ when $n = 4$.

For $n \ge 5$, by considering $A = \{-1, 0, \varepsilon, 2\varepsilon, \dots, (n-4)\varepsilon, 1 + (n-4)\varepsilon, 1 + (n-3)\varepsilon\}$ where $\varepsilon > 0$, we need $M \le 3 + f(n)\varepsilon$ for some function $f$. When $\varepsilon$ tends to $0$, this yields $M \le 3$.

Firstly, for $n = 5$, let $a < b < c < d < e$ be the elements in $A$. Since $c-a > c-b$ and $e-c > d-c$, we must have $c-a \ge 1$ and $e-c \ge 1$ by considering the element $c$. Also, at least one of $c-b \ge 1$ and $d-c \ge 1$ must hold. WLOG assume $c-b \ge 1$. Then we have
$$
S \ge (|a| + |e|) + (|b| + |c|) \ge |e-a| + |c-b| \ge 2 + 1 = 3.
$$
This shows the largest $M$ is $3$ when $n = 5$.

Secondly, for $n \ge 6$, let $a$ be the element with the smallest absolute value. WLOG assume $a \ge 0$.

* If $0 \le a \le \frac{1}{2}$, then whenever $|x-a| \ge 1$, we must have $|x| \ge 1-a$. Since there are three such $x$'s, we have $S \ge 3(1-a) + (n-3)a \ge 3(1-a) + 3a = 3$.
* If $a > \frac{1}{2}$, then all elements have absolute value larger than $\frac{1}{2}$, and hence $S > \frac{n}{2} \ge 3$.

This shows the largest $M$ is $3$ when $n \ge 6$.

problem_md: A two digit number $s$ is *special* if $s$ is the common two leading digits of the decimal expansion of $4^n$ and $5^n$, where $n$ is a certain positive integer. Given that there are two special numbers, find these two special numbers.
solution_md:
The condition on $s$ means there exists a positive integer $n$ and nonnegative integers $a$ and $b$ such that
$$
10^a s \le 5^n < 10^a (s+1) \quad \text{and} \quad 10^b s \le 4^n < 10^b (s+1).
$$
Note that $10^a s = 5^n$ and $10^b s = 4^n$ cannot hold simultaneously. Multiplying the second inequality and the square of the first inequality, we obtain
$$
10^{2a+b}s^3 < 5^{2n}4^n = 10^{2n} < 10^{2a+b}(s+1)^3.
$$
This implies $s^3 < 10^{2n-2a-b} < (s+1)^3$. Since $s$ is a two-digit number, we have
$$
10^3 \le s^3 < 10^{2n-2a-b} < (s+1)^3 \le 10^6.
$$
Thus, $2n-2a-b$ is equal to $4$ or $5$. This shows $s = \lfloor 10^{\frac{4}{3}} \rfloor = 21$ or $\lfloor 10^{\frac{5}{3}} \rfloor = 46$. The two special numbers are $21$ and $46$.

problem_md: Find all functions $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ satisfying the following condition: for any real numbers $x$ and $y$, the number $f(x + f(y))$ is equal to $x + f(y)$ or $f(f(x)) + y$.
solution_md:
$f(x) = x + c$ for some constant $c$.

We first check that all such functions are solutions. Indeed, if $f(x) = x + c$, then
$$
f(x + f(y)) = f(x + y + c) = x + y + 2c = f(f(x)) + y
$$
for any $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$.

Next, we prove that these are the only solutions. Replacing $x$ by $x - f(y)$, the given condition means
$$
f(x) = x \quad \text{or} \quad f(x) = f(f(x - f(y))) + y
$$
for any $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$. We are done if $f(x) = x$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$. Assume $f(a) \neq a$ for some $a \in \mathbb{R}$. Then we have
$$
f(a) = f(f(a - f(y))) + y \quad (1)
$$
for any $y \in \mathbb{R}$. Note that $f$ is injective, since if $f(\alpha) = f(\beta)$, then
$$
\alpha = f(\alpha) - f(f(\alpha - f(\alpha))) = f(\alpha) - f(f(\alpha - f(\beta))) = \beta
$$
by (1). Setting $y = 0$ in (1), we have $f(a) = f(f(a - f(0)))$, and hence
$$
a = f(a - f(0)) \quad (2)
$$
as $f$ is injective. In particular, this implies $f(0) \neq 0$ (as $f(a) \neq a$).

* If $f(x) \neq x$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$, then (2) holds for all $a \in \mathbb{R}$. Replacing $a$ by $x + f(0)$, this gives $f(x) = x + c$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$, where $c = f(0)$ is a constant as desired.

* Suppose $f(b) = b$ for some $b$. Note that $f(f(a)) \neq f(a)$ since $f(a) \neq a$ and $f$ is injective. It follows that (1) holds if we replace $a$ by $f(a)$. Thus, we obtain
$$
f(f(a)) = f(f(f(a) - f(y))) + y.
$$
Putting $y = a$, this yields
$$
f(f(a)) = f(f(0)) + a. \quad (3)
$$
Next, putting $y = b$ in (1), we have $f(a) = f(f(a - b)) + b$. Recall that $f(a) \neq a$, so we cannot have $f(a - b) = a - b$. Therefore, using (3) with $a$ replaced by $a - b$, we deduce
$$
f(a) = f(f(a - b)) + b = f(f(0)) + (a - b) + b = a + f(f(0)). \quad (4)
$$
Recall that $f(0) \neq 0$. Thus, we may take $a = 0$ in (4). This implies $f(0) = f(f(0))$. But then this yields $0 = f(0)$ by the injectivity, which is a contradiction.

problem_md:
Let $x$, $y$, $z$ be real numbers such that $x + y + z \neq 0$. Find the minimum value of
$$
\frac{|x| + |x + 4y| + |y + 7z| + 2|z|}{|x + y + z|}
$$
solution_md:
By the triangle inequality, we have
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{|x| + |x + 4y| + |y + 7z| + 2|z|}{|x + y + z|} &\ge \frac{|x| + \frac{4}{11}|x + 4y| + \frac{1}{11}|-y - 7z| + 2|z|}{|x + y + z|} \\
&\ge \frac{|x + \frac{4}{11}(x + 4y) + \frac{1}{11}(-y - 7z) + 2z|}{|x + y + z|} \\
&= \frac{15}{11}.
\end{aligned}
$$
Equality holds when $(x, y, z) = (28k, -7k, k)$ for some $k \neq 0$. So the minimum value is $\frac{15}{11}$.

problem_md:
After expanding the polynomial $(1 + x + y)^{2023}$ and collecting like terms, we obtain the expression
$$
a_0 + a_1x + a_2y + a_3x^2 + a_4xy + a_5y^2 + \dots + a_ky^{2023}
$$
Find the total number of $a_i$'s which are divisible by $5$.
solution_md:
Note that $(1+x+y)^5 \equiv 1+x^5+y^5 \pmod{5}$ since the coefficients of all other terms are of the form $\frac{5!}{i!(5-i)!}$ for some $i, j$ satisfying $0 \le i+j \le 5$ and $(i, j) \ne (0, 0), (0, 5), (5, 0)$, all of which are divisible by $5$ as the denominator is not divisible by $5$. Inductively, we find that $(1+x+y)^{5k} \equiv 1+x^{5k}+y^{5k} \pmod{5}$ for all positive integers $k$. Now, note that $2023 = 3 \cdot 5^4 + 5^3 + 4 \cdot 5 + 3$. Therefore, we deduce
$$
(1+x+y)^{2023} = (1+x+y)^{3 \cdot 5^4} (1+x+y)^{5^3} (1+x+y)^{4 \cdot 5} (1+x+y)^3 \\
\equiv (1+x^{5^4}+y^{5^4})^3 (1+x^{5^3}+y^{5^3}) (1+x^{5^2}+y^{5^2}) (1+x^{5^1}+y^3) \pmod{5}. \quad (3)
$$
When we expand $(1 + X + Y)^n$, each term is of the form $X^a Y^b$ where $0 \le a+b \le n$. The total number of terms in the expansion is the number of nonnegative integer solutions to $a+b+c=n$ (by defining $c=n-a-b$), which is equal to $\binom{n+2}{2}$. Therefore, there are $\binom{5}{2} = 10$, $\binom{3}{2} = 3$, $\binom{6}{2} = 15$ and $\binom{5}{2} = 10$ terms in the expansions of the factors on the right of (3) respectively. When we further expand their product, there are no like terms because the factorization corresponds to the base $5$ representation of the exponents (for example, the term $x^{5^4}y^{3 \cdot 5+2}$ can only be obtained by taking $x^{5^4}$ from the first factor, taking $1$ from the second factor, taking $y^{3 \cdot 5}$ from the third factor and taking $y^2$ from the fourth factor). Also, the coefficient of every term is not divisible by $5$ because all exponents are less than $5$, and $5$ is a prime number. Thus, there are
$$
10 \times 3 \times 15 \times 10 = 4500
$$
coefficients $a_i$'s which are not divisible by $5$. The total number of $a_i$'s which are divisible by $5$ is
$$
\left(\frac{2025}{2}\right) - 4500 = 2044800.
$$

problem_md: A test question reads 'write down three consecutive positive integers not exceeding $2023$ in ascending order, such that one of them is a multiple of $6$, another one is a multiple of $7$ and the remaining one is a multiple of $8$. How many different correct answers are there for this question?'
solution_md: Let $n$, $n + 1$ and $n + 2$ be the three consecutive positive integers. Then $1 \leq n \leq 2021$. Since multiples of $6$ and $8$ must be even, we must have $n + 1$ being divisible by $7$ whereas $n$ and $n + 2$ are divisible by $6$ and $8$ in either order. For each of these two cases, there are exactly two choices of $n$ among any $168$ consecutive positive integers by the Chinese remainder theorem since the L.C.M. of $6$, $7$ and $8$ is $168$. As $2021 = 12 \times 168 + 5$, and none of $n = 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020, 2021$ satisfies the conditions, there are $2 \times 12 = 24$ choices of $n$.

title: ķ”ÖµĖ» ???? #461
problem_md: Find all positive integer(s) $n$ such that $(2^n - 1)(5^n - 1)$ is a perfect square.
solution_md:
$n = 1$.
When $n = 1$, $(2^1 - 1)(5^1 - 1) = 4$ is a perfect square. Suppose $n > 1$.

* If $n \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$, we have $(2^n - 1)(5^n - 1) \equiv (2)(-1) \equiv 3 \pmod{5}$, which is not true for a square.

* If $n \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, we rewrite
$$
(2^n - 1)(5^n - 1) = (2^n - 1) \cdot 4(5^{n-1} + 5^{n-2} + \dots + 1).
$$
If this is a square, so is $(2^n - 1)(5^{n-1} + 5^{n-2} + \dots + 1)$. However,
$$
(2^n - 1)(5^{n-1} + 5^{n-2} + \dots + 1) \equiv (-1)(1 + 1 + \dots + 1) = -n \equiv 3 \pmod{4},
$$
which is not true for a square.

* If $n = 2m$ is even, suppose $(2^n - 1)(5^n - 1)$ is a perfect square. Let $2^n - 1 = ds^2$ and $5^n - 1 = dt^2$ where $d$ is squarefree. We first show that $d > 1$. Indeed, if $d = 1$, then $5^{2m} - t^2 = 1$. This gives $(5^m - t)(5^m + t) = 1$, which is impossible as $t > 0$. Now, it is known that the solutions to Pell's equation $x^2 - dy^2 = 1$ are generated by $(x_1 + y_1\sqrt{d})^n = x_n + y_n\sqrt{d}$, where $(x_1, y_1)$ is the fundamental solution.
Claim. Let $(x_k, y_k)$ be a solution to $x^2 - dy^2 = 1$.
(i) If $k \equiv 0 \pmod{2}$, then $x_k \equiv 1 \pmod{2}$ and $y_k \equiv 0 \pmod{2}$.
(ii) If $k \equiv 1 \pmod{2}$, then $x_k \equiv x_1 \pmod{2}$ and $y_k \equiv y_1 \pmod{2}$.

Proof.
(i) Let $k = 2l$. Then we have
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_k + y_k \sqrt{d} &= (x_1 + y_1 \sqrt{d})^{2l} \\
&= (x_l + y_l \sqrt{d})^2 \\
&= x_l^2 + dy_l^2 + 2x_l y_l \sqrt{d} \\
&= 2x_l^2 - 1 + 2x_l y_l \sqrt{d}.
\end{aligned}
$$
So $x_k = 2x_l^2 - 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{2}$ and $y_k = 2x_l y_l \equiv 0 \pmod{2}$.

$$
x_{2l+1} = x_{2l}x_1 + y_{2l}y_1 d \equiv x_1 \pmod{2},
$$
$$
y_{2l+1} = x_{2l}y_1 + x_1 y_{2l} \equiv y_1 \pmod{2}.
$$
$\Box$
Now, since $2^{2m} - 1 = ds^2$, $(x, y) = (2^m, s)$ is a solution to $x^2 - dy^2 = 1$. By the claim, we must have $x_1 \equiv 0 \pmod{2}$. Similarly, since $5^{2m} - 1 = dt^2$, $(x_k, y_k) = (5^m, t)$ is another solution. By the claim, as $5^m \not\equiv x_1 \pmod{2}$, we must have $k \equiv 0 \pmod{2}$. Let $k = 2l$. Then we have $5^m = x_{2l} = 2x_l^2 - 1$. This implies $2x_l^2 - 1 \equiv 0 \pmod{5}$, and hence $x_l^2 \equiv 3 \pmod{5}$. This is impossible.
Therefore, the only solution is $n = 1$.

problem_md:
Find all nonnegative real numbers $a$, $b$, $c$ such that
$$
\frac{4a + 9b + 25c}{2a + 3b + 5c} + \frac{4b + 9c + 25a}{2b + 3c + 5a} + \frac{4c + 9a + 25b}{2c + 3a + 5b} = 10
$$
solution_md:
By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, we have
$$
(a + b + c)(4a + 9b + 25c) \geq (2a + 3b + 5c)^2.
$$
This implies $\frac{4a + 9b + 25c}{2a + 3b + 5c} \geq \frac{2a + 3b + 5c}{a + b + c}$. Adding similar inequalities, we obtain
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{4a + 9b + 25c}{2a + 3b + 5c} + \frac{4b + 9c + 25a}{2b + 3c + 5a} + \frac{4c + 9a + 25b}{2c + 3a + 5b} \\
\geq & \frac{4a + 9b + 25c}{a + b + c} + \frac{4b + 9c + 25a}{b + c + a} + \frac{4c + 9a + 25b}{c + a + b} \\
= & \frac{10a + 10b + 10c}{a + b + c} \\
= & 10.
\end{aligned}
$$

By the given condition, the equality should hold. This means we need
$$
a : b : c = 4a : 9b : 25c = 25a : 4b : 9c = 9a : 25b : 4c,
$$
which means two of $a$, $b$, $c$ are zero, and the remaining can be any positive real number.

problem_md: Let $ABCD$ be a convex quadrilateral with $AB = 5$, $AD = 17$, and $CD = 6$. If the angle bisectors of $\angle BAD$ and $\angle ADC$ intersect at the midpoint of $BC$, find the area of $ABCD$.
solution_md:
Let $M$ be the midpoint of $BC$. Let $B'$ and $C'$ be points on $AD$ such that $AB' = AB = 5$ and $DC' = DC = 6$. Then $B'C' = 17 - 5 - 6 = 6$. Note that $\triangle ABM \cong \triangle AB'M$ and $\triangle DCM \cong \triangle DC'M$. Note also that $\triangle MB'C'$ is isosceles as $MB' = MB = MC = MC'$. Let $N$ be the midpoint of $B'C'$. Then we have $B'N = C'N = 3$ and $\angle MB'C' = \angle MC'B'$. Observe that $MCDB'$ is cyclic since $MD$ bisects $\angle B'DC$ and $MB' = MC$ (while $DB' \ne DC$). Thus,
$$
2 \angle B'MA = \angle B'MB = \angle C'DC = 2 \angle MDC'.
$$
![](attached_image_1.png)

Finally we have $MN = \sqrt{MC'^2 - NC'^2} = \sqrt{30-9} = \sqrt{21}$. Therefore,
$$
\begin{align*} 
[ABCD] &= 2([AB'M] + [DC'M] + [MB'N]) \\ 
&= (AB' + C'D + B'N) \times MN \\ 
&= (5+6+3)\sqrt{21} = 14\sqrt{21}. 
\end{align*}
$$

problem_md: $ABCD$ is a square of side length $1$. $BC$ is extended to $E$ and $DC$ is extended to $F$ such that $BE = DF = 3$. The circumcircle of $\triangle AEF$ meets the extensions of $CB$ and $CD$ at $G$ and $H$ respectively. Find $GH$.
solution_md:
Answer: $\frac{3\sqrt{2}}{2}$

Note that $AG = AH$ by symmetry. Hence they subtend equal angles on the circumference of the circle, i.e. we have $\angle AFG = \angle AFH$. Suppose $FA$ meets $GE$ at $I$.

By similar triangles, we have $BI : IC = AB : CF = 1 : 2$. Hence $IC = \frac{2}{3}$. Let $GC = x$. Using the angle bisector theorem, we have $GI : IC = FG : FC$, i.e.

$$
\frac{x - \frac{2}{3}}{\frac{2}{3}} = \frac{\sqrt{x^2 + 2^2}}{2}
$$

Solving gives $x = \frac{3}{2}$. By symmetry, $HC$ also has length $\frac{3}{2}$ and so $GH = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{2}$.

title: ķ”ÖµĖ» ???? #465
problem_md: Let $n$ be a positive integer such that $1^3 + 2^3 + \dots + n^3$ is divisible by $n+3$. Find the greatest possible value of $n$.
solution_md:
Answer: 15
Note that
$$
1^3 + 2^3 + \cdots + n^3 = \frac{n^2(n+1)^2}{4}
$$

Hence, for $n + 3$ to divide $1^3 + 2^3 + \cdots + n^3$, it is necessary for $n^2(n + 1)^2$ to be a multiple of $n + 3$, or equivalently, by setting $m = n + 3$,
$$
0 \equiv n^2(n+1)^2 = (m-3)^2(m-2)^2 \equiv 36 \pmod{m}.
$$
Hence $m$ must be a factor of 36. To find the greatest possible value of $n$, we proceed as follows:
* If $m = 36$ (i.e. $n = 33$), we have $1^3 + 2^3 + \cdots + 33^3 = \frac{33^2 \cdot 34^2}{4} = 33^2 \cdot 17^2$ which is odd (and hence not divisible by $n + 3 = 36$).
* The next largest possible value of $m$ is 18 (corresponding to $n = 15$), in which case we have
$$
1^3 + 2^3 + \cdots + 15^3 = \frac{15^2 \cdot 16^2}{4} = 15^2 \cdot 8^2 = 2^6 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5^2,
$$
which is divisible by $n + 3 = 2 \cdot 3^2$.
It follows that the answer is 15.

problem_md: Let $\Omega$ be the incircle of $\triangle ABC$. There are two smaller circles $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ inside $\triangle ABC$. The circle $\omega_1$ is tangent to $\Omega$ at $P$, tangent to $BC$ at $D$, and also tangent to $AB$. The circle $\omega_2$ is tangent to $\Omega$ at $Q$, tangent to $BC$ at $E$, and also tangent to $AC$. Prove that $D, E, Q, P$ are concyclic.
solution_md:
Let $S$ be the point on $\Omega$ different from the contact point of $\Omega$ and $BC$ such that its tangent to $\Omega$ is parallel to $BC$. Then the tangent at $D$ to $\omega_1$ is parallel to the tangent at $S$ to $\Omega$. By considering the homothety with centre $P$ mapping $\omega_1$ to $\Omega$, we see that $D$, $P$, $S$ are collinear. Similarly, $E$, $Q$, $S$ are collinear. Let $X$ be a point on the tangent at $S$ to $\Omega$ as shown. Then we have
$$
\angle SQP = \angle PSX = \angle EDP.
$$
This implies $D$, $E$, $Q$, $P$ are concyclic.

problem_md: In $\triangle ABC$, $\angle BAC = 150^\circ$ and $BC = 74$. $D$ is a point on $BC$ such that $BD = 14$ and $\angle ADB = 60^\circ$. Find the area of $\triangle ABC$.
solution_md:
Let $b$, $c$ and $d$ be the lengths of $AB$, $AC$ and $AD$ respectively.
The area of $\triangle ABC$ is $\frac{1}{2}bc\sin 150^\circ$. Alternatively, using $BC$ as base, the height of the triangle is $d\sin 60^\circ$ and so the area is also equal to $\frac{1}{2}(74)d\sin 60^\circ$. This gives
$$
\frac{1}{2}bc\sin 150^\circ = \frac{1}{2}(74)d\sin 60^\circ,
$$
which simplifies to $bc = 74\sqrt{3}d$. Using this and the cosine formula, we have
$$
\begin{aligned}
74^2 &= b^2 + c^2 - 2bc \cos 150^\circ \\
&= [d^2 + 14^2 - 2d(14) \cos 60^\circ] + [d^2 + 60^2 - 2d(60) \cos 120^\circ] - 2(74\sqrt{3}d) \cos 150^\circ \\
&= 2d^2 + 268d + 3796,
\end{aligned}
$$
which simplifies to $2(d - 6)(d + 140) = 0$. Hence $d = 6$ and so the area of $\triangle ABC$ is $\frac{1}{2}(74)d\sin 60^\circ = 111\sqrt{3}$.

problem_md: How many solutions are there to the equation $\log_3 x = 3 \sin(3\pi x)$?
solution_md:
Answer: 81
A sketch of the graphs of $y = \log_3 x$ and $y = 3 \sin (3\pi x)$ is as follows:

![](attached_image_1.png)

Note that the right hand side of the equation (i.e. $3\sin(3\pi x)$) must be between $-3$ and $3$. Hence any solution satisfies $-3 \le \log_3 x \le 3$, or $\frac{1}{27} \le x \le 27$. There are three solutions for $x \le 1$ as shown in the graph above. For $x \in (1, 27]$, we have $\log_3 x > 0$ and the function $3\sin(3\pi x)$ is positive in 39 intervals, namely, $(\frac{4}{3}, \frac{5}{3})$, $(\frac{6}{3}, \frac{7}{3})$, ..., $(\frac{80}{3}, \frac{81}{3})$. In each of these intervals the graph of $\log_3 x > 0$ intersects the graph of $3\sin(3\pi x)$ at exactly two points. The answer is thus $3 + 39(2) = 81$.

title: ķ”ÖµĖ» ???? #469
problem_md: Find the largest real root to the equation $\sqrt[3]{x^3 + 3x^2 - 4} - x = \sqrt[3]{x^3 - 3x + 2} - 1$.
solution_md:
Answer: $\frac{1+3\sqrt{5}}{4}$
We can factorize the expressions to get
$$
\sqrt[3]{(x-1)(x+2)^2} - x = \sqrt[3]{(x-1)^2(x+2)} - 1.
$$
If we let $u = \sqrt[3]{x-1}$ and $v = \sqrt[3]{x+2}$, the equation becomes $uv^2 = u^2v + u^3$, or
$$
u(u^2 + uv - v^2) = 0.$$
If $u = 0$, then $x = 1$. If there is another root $x > 1$, then $u, v$ must both be positive, so $u^2 + uv - v^2 = 0$ gives $u = \lambda v$ where $\lambda = \frac{1}{2}(-1 + \sqrt{5})$. Hence $u^3 = (\lambda v)^3$, i.e. $x - 1 = \lambda^3(x + 2)$, so we have
$$
x = \frac{1 + 2\lambda^3}{1 - \lambda^3} = \frac{1 + 2(\sqrt{5} - 2)}{1 - (\sqrt{5} - 2)} = \frac{1 + 3\sqrt{5}}{4}.
$$
It is easy to check that such $x$ indeed satisfies the original equation, and hence is the largest real root to the equation.
